Send a request like: `https://api2.blockchain.info/charts/total-bitcoins?cors=true&format=csv&lang=en%0AX-XSS-Protection:0%0AContent-Type:text/html%0A` to inject a JavaScript payload in the body by setting `Content-Type` and disabling XSS protection.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify HTTP requests, observe response headers.
OWASP ZAP: Similar functionality to Burp Suite for request interception and modification.
success_indicators: Response headers contain new, attacker-controlled headers (e.g., `TEST`).
Response headers indicate a change in content type (e.g., `Content-Type: text/html`).
Execution of injected JavaScript code in the browser (e.g., `alert(document.domain)`).
failure_indicators: The injected CRLF characters are not interpreted as line breaks and are reflected literally in the response.
Input sanitization filters out or fails to process the `%0D%0A` sequence.
The server returns an error response (e.g., 400 Bad Request) indicating invalid input.

playbooks.impact: Ability to inject arbitrary HTTP headers, which can lead to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) by altering the Content-Type to HTML and injecting a JavaScript payload, potentially leading to session hijacking, unauthorized actions, or phishing.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test URL parameters for CRLF injection by appending URL-encoded carriage return and line feed characters (%0D%0A), especially when these parameters are reflected in HTTP response headers, as this can lead to various injection vulnerabilities like XSS.

meta.title: Accessing Localhost via Vhost

tech_stack_fingerprints: HTTP 200 OK response for common vhost names

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web server hosts multiple websites using virtual hosts, and an attacker can successfully enumerate 'localhost' as a virtual host, revealing sensitive information.
prerequisites: Target web server is configured to host multiple virtual hosts.
Target web server responds to requests for 'localhost' as a virtual host.
applicability_signals: Ability to get a 200 OK response for 'localhost' as a virtual host.
Directory listing is enabled on the 'localhost' virtual host.
skip_if: Web server does not respond to 'localhost' virtual host requests.
Directory listing is disabled and no sensitive files are directly accessible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate common virtual host names (e.g., app, beta, admin, webmail, localhost) against the target IP address using tools like virtual-host-discovery, pentest-tools, or nmap http-vhosts script.
Step 2: Manually check for common vhost names like 'localhost' by sending HTTP requests with the 'Host' header set to 'localhost'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send an HTTP GET request to the target IP address with the 'Host' header set to 'localhost'. Example: `curl -H 'Host: localhost' <TARGET_URL>`
Step 2: Analyze the response for a '200 OK' status code and check if directory listing is enabled.
Step 3: If directory listing is enabled, navigate through the directories to identify and download sensitive files.
Step 4: Extract sensitive information such as database credentials, API keys, email addresses, etc.
tools_recommended: curl for manual requests
virtual-host-discovery (https://github.com/jobertabma/virtual-host-discovery)
pentest-tools (https://pentest-tools.com/information-gathering/find-virtual-hosts)
nmap http-vhosts script (https://nmap.org/nsedoc/scripts/http-vhosts.html)
success_indicators: HTTP 200 OK response when requesting 'Host: localhost'.
Directory listing is visible and allows access to files.
Discovery of sensitive configuration files (.yml, .php, .xml, .json) or credentials (MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD, API_KEYS, etc.).
failure_indicators: 404 Not Found or other error codes when requesting 'Host: localhost' → Try other common vhost names or use a different enumeration strategy.
200 OK response but no directory listing and no obvious sensitive files → The vhost might be misconfigured but not directly exploitable for information disclosure in this manner.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive information such as database credentials, API keys, email addresses, internal network information, and other configuration details, potentially leading to further compromise of the application or server.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always enumerate virtual hosts, including 'localhost', as they may expose sensitive configurations or internal applications with weaker security controls.

meta.title: How I hacked Google’s bug tracking system itself for $15,600 in bounties

affected_components: issue tracking system, specifically starring functionality

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can interact with issues in the Google Issue Tracker, particularly when starring or subscribing to issues.
prerequisites: An authenticated user account on the Google Issue Tracker.
applicability_signals: Ability to view or interact with issue threads.
Presence of a 'star' or 'subscribe' functionality for issues.
No apparent access control checks when attempting to star issues that the user might not have access to.
skip_if: No authenticated access to the Issue Tracker is possible.
All starring/subscription attempts on inaccessible issues are properly rejected with access denied errors.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access the Google Issue Tracker web interface and observe the UI for functionalities related to issue management and notifications.
Step 2: Identify the 'star' functionality or similar mechanisms that subscribe users to issue updates.
Step 3: Examine the request/response when starring an issue to understand the underlying API calls and parameters.
Step 4: Attempt to star issues for which the user does not have explicit access (e.g., private issues, vulnerability reports not shared with the user) to test access controls.
attack_steps: Step 1: Log in to the Google Issue Tracker with an authenticated user account.
Step 2: Identify a known issue ID (or a range of issue IDs) that the attacker account is not supposed to have access to.
Step 3: Send an HTTP request to star the identified issue ID(s) using the 'star' functionality, even if access should be restricted.
Step 4: Monitor the attacker's registered email address for notifications related to comments or updates on the starred issue.
payloads_or_patterns: HTTP POST request to the endpoint responsible for starring issues, with the issue ID as a parameter.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests when starring issues, and to analyze responses.
success_indicators: Receiving email notifications for comments or updates on issues that the attacker account should not have access to.
The UI confirming that the issue has been starred, despite lack of explicit access.
failure_indicators: Receiving a '403 Forbidden' or similar access denied error when attempting to star an issue.
No notification emails are received for updates on starred issues that should be inaccessible.
The UI does not confirm that the issue has been starred.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain visibility into internal Google issues, including potentially confidential vulnerability reports, that they are not intended to see. This allows them to monitor ongoing security research, internal discussions, and potentially exploit vulnerabilities before they are fixed by obtaining early intelligence.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify access control for functionalities that subscribe or notify users about content, as failures can lead to unintended information disclosure of sensitive or private data.

meta.title: Slack SAML authentication bypass

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when the target application uses SAML for authentication and allows the submission of SAML assertions.
prerequisites: Attacker has access to a valid SAML assertion, even if it is expired or intended for a different Service Provider (e.g., Github).
applicability_signals: Presence of a SAML endpoint (e.g., /sso/saml) that accepts POST requests with SAML assertions.
skip_if: The SAML endpoint performs strict validation on the Audience and AudienceRestriction elements, rejecting assertions not intended for Slack.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify SAML endpoints used by Slack. Common endpoints include /sso/saml or similar paths related to single sign-on.
Step 2: Confirm that these endpoints accept POST requests containing SAML assertions in the request body.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain a SAML assertion intended for a different Service Provider. An old, expired assertion from a service like Github is ideal.
Step 2: Modify the SAML assertion if necessary, ensuring it contains an AudienceRestriction element, even if it's for the wrong Service Provider.
Step 3: Send a POST request to Slack's SAML endpoint (e.g., POST /sso/saml) with the crafted SAML assertion in the request body.
Step 4: Observe the response. If authentication bypass is successful, the attacker will be logged into Slack as the user associated with the provided SAML assertion.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /sso/saml HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

SAMLResponse=<SAML_ASSERTION_STRING>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (with SAML Raider extension for assertion editing)
success_indicators: Successful login into Slack with a username that does not belong to the attacker, associated with the identity in the provided SAML assertion.
HTTP 200 OK response with a session cookie or redirection to the user's dashboard after submitting the crafted SAML assertion.
failure_indicators: HTTP 400 Bad Request or 403 Forbidden response indicating the SAML assertion was rejected.
Error messages specifically mentioning invalid audience or assertion format. -> Pivot: Investigate other SAML parameters or look for alternative authentication bypass vectors.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can bypass authentication and gain access to a Slack workspace as an arbitrary user, potentially leading to data exfiltration, unauthorized actions within the workspace, and reputational damage.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the Audience and AudienceRestriction elements within SAML assertions to ensure they are intended for the current Service Provider before establishing a user session.

meta.title: DOM XSS – auth.uber.com

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application uses a 'next_url' parameter for redirects after login and has insufficient validation on the scheme and content of this parameter.
prerequisites: Target application redirects unauthenticated users to auth.uber.com for login.
The login endpoint accepts a 'next_url' parameter.
The 'next_url' parameter is not sufficiently validated for its scheme or content.
applicability_signals: Login redirect URL contains a 'next_url' parameter.
Attempts to inject custom schemes (like 'javascript:') into 'next_url' are not blocked by server-side filtering.
The 'next_url' parameter appears to be used directly in a client-side redirect or in a response header.
skip_if: All schemes other than http/https are blocked by server-side validation.
The 'next_url' parameter is only validated against a strict whitelist of known Uber subdomains.
JavaScript execution is heavily sandboxed and cannot be triggered via the 'next_url' parameter.

recon_steps: Visit auth.uber.com without authentication to observe the login redirect behavior.
Identify the 'next_url' parameter in the redirect URL.
Attempt to change the domain in 'next_url' to an external domain to check for Open Redirect vulnerabilities.
attack_steps: Step 1: Analyze the 'next_url' parameter for validation bypasses, focusing on the scheme.
Step 2: Try injecting non-standard schemes like 'javascript://' and observe the server's response (e.g., Location header). Note case sensitivity bypasses (e.g., 'jaVAscript://').
Step 3: If standard JavaScript URI schemes are blocked, fuzz for alternative URI schemes like 'data:' that can execute JavaScript.
Step 4: Construct a 'data:' URI payload containing HTML and a `<script>` tag with `window.location` to redirect to a controlled domain or execute JavaScript.
Step 5: Craft the full URL using the 'next_url' parameter with the data URI payload and observe the redirect behavior.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, observe redirect headers, and fuzz parameters.
Manual browser inspection: To analyze JavaScript execution and DOM manipulation.
success_indicators: The browser successfully redirects to the domain specified within the data URI payload (e.g., reddit.com in the writeup).
JavaScript code within the data URI payload executes, e.g., `alert(1)` or `console.log('XSS')`.
failure_indicators: 400 Bad Request or other error responses when using non-standard schemes in 'next_url'.
The redirect fails, or the `next_url` parameter is stripped or encoded differently than expected.
JavaScript code is not executed, or the script execution is not in the origin of auth.uber.com.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can redirect users to arbitrary external domains by manipulating the 'next_url' parameter, potentially leading to phishing attacks or the execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the user's browser (though not necessarily in the auth.uber.com origin if not properly exploited). In this specific writeup, the Data URI scheme bypass allowed for redirecting the user to an arbitrary site using `window.location`, but the writeup states the JS was not running in the origin of auth.uber.com, implying a potential limitation for direct XSS on auth.uber.com itself.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate both the scheme and the host of redirect URLs, especially when they are controlled by user input, and be aware of alternative URI schemes like 'data:' that can bypass sanitization filters.

meta.title: Adobe ColdFusion Deserialization RCE (CVE-2017-11283, CVE-2017-11284)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target exposes a Java RMI service, specifically the `coldfusion.flex.rmi.DataServicesCFProxy` interface, and the bundled Java runtime environment has not been adequately patched against deserialization vulnerabilities.
prerequisites: Target is running Adobe ColdFusion and exposes RMI services.
Target's JRE is vulnerable to deserialization attacks via common libraries (e.g., older versions of Apache Commons Collections or Rhino).
applicability_signals: Observable RMI endpoints on the target.
Presence of `coldfusion.flex.rmi.DataServicesCFProxy` interface in RMI services.
ColdFusion version is not updated to patch CVE-2017-11283 and CVE-2017-11284, or the JRE itself is not manually updated.
Absence of WAF blocking RMI traffic.
skip_if: RMI ports are not open or reachable.
Target is running a fully patched ColdFusion and updated JRE that mitigates known deserialization gadgets.
RMI traffic is blocked by network firewalls or WAFs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan target IP addresses and ports for open RMI registry (port 1099 by default).
Step 2: Use tools like BaRMIe or RMI enumeration scripts to identify active RMI services and their interfaces.
Step 3: Look for services exposing `coldfusion.flex.rmi.DataServicesCFProxy`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a Java deserialization payload using a tool like ysoserial, targeting known vulnerable Java libraries (e.g., Apache Commons Collections, Mozilla Rhino). The choice of gadget will depend on the libraries available in the target's ColdFusion JRE.
Step 2: Connect to the identified RMI endpoint.
Step 3: Invoke one of the methods on the `DataServicesCFProxy` interface (e.g., `fill`, `sync`, `get`, `count`, `fillContains`) and pass the crafted deserialization payload as a parameter.
Step 4: Observe the target's response or side effects for signs of code execution.
payloads_or_patterns: ysoserial generated payload for vulnerable Java libraries (e.g., CommonsCollections, Resort, Rome, etc. depending on ColdFusion JRE version).
tools_recommended: BaRMIe: For RMI enumeration and interaction.
ysoserial: For generating Java deserialization payloads.
Burp Suite: To capture and analyze RMI traffic if proxied, or for scripting.
success_indicators: Successful callback to an attacker-controlled server (e.g., DNS or HTTP interaction if payload includes network communication).
Arbitrary command execution observed on the target system (e.g., reverse shell established, specific file created/modified).
Unexpected error messages or application behavior indicative of code execution.
failure_indicators: RMI connection refused or timed out → Target RMI service is not exposed or blocked by firewall.
Deserialization error without code execution → Payload gadget not effective for target's JRE, or WAF is inspecting RMI traffic.
Unexpected RMI method response without code execution → Payload may be malformed or gadget is not working as expected.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Adobe ColdFusion server, running with the privileges of the ColdFusion service account (often SYSTEM by default on Windows). This allows attackers to take full control of the server, access sensitive data, deploy malware, or pivot to other systems within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test RMI services for deserialization vulnerabilities by attempting to pass crafted Java objects to exposed methods, as these can lead to unauthenticated RCE if the target's JRE is not adequately patched.

meta.title: Uber Bug Bounty: Gaining Access To An Internal Chat System

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses SAML for authentication and the SAML implementation does not properly validate the signature of SAML assertions.
prerequisites: Discovering a SAML-based authentication endpoint for a target subdomain.
applicability_signals: The presence of a '/login/sso/saml' endpoint.
The authentication flow involves redirecting to an SSO provider (like OneLogin).
skip_if: The SAML endpoint consistently returns errors or blocks requests for malformed SAML assertions before processing.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks POST requests to the SAML endpoint.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate subdomains of the target organization (e.g., using crt.sh with wildcard like %.uberinternal.com) to find potential internal applications.
Step 2: Access discovered subdomains and observe authentication mechanisms. Look for SSO prompts or redirects to SAML endpoints.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the SAML assertion endpoint (e.g., /login/sso/saml).
Step 2: Construct a basic SAML assertion XML. Crucially, this assertion should be crafted *without* a signature to test signature validation.
Step 3: Send a POST request to the identified SAML endpoint with the unsigned SAML assertion as the request body.
Step 4: Observe the server's response. If the server processes the unsigned assertion and grants access (indicated by a redirect or session cookie), the vulnerability is confirmed.
